# ARC-QUIC-RFC9001-0004 - QUIC RFC 9001 Post-Stop Stateless Reset Response Matrix Architecture

## Purpose

Define the design boundary for the retained-route stateless-reset response floor after AEAD-limit terminal discard, including exact retained-route lookup, same-remote-address-and-port ownership, loop-prevention, and emission-rate suppression.

## Requirements Satisfied

- REQ-QUIC-RFC9001-S6P6-0006

## Design Summary

The runtime already has a shared retained-route lookup helper for post-stop stateless-reset response. This design keeps that helper exact: later packets only qualify when they resolve to a retained route bound to a remembered token for the same remote address and port, and the endpoint helper still suppresses emission when loop-prevention or rate-budget gates fail. The connected endpoint host and listener host continue to delegate through the same helper so direct emission and receive-loop response ownership stay aligned instead of growing separate response models.

## Key Components

- src/Incursa.Quic/QuicConnectionRuntimeEndpoint.cs
- src/Incursa.Quic/QuicConnectionEndpointHost.cs
- src/Incursa.Quic/QuicListenerHost.cs
- src/Incursa.Quic/QuicStatelessReset.cs
- tests/Incursa.Quic.Tests/RequirementHomes/RFC9001/REQ-QUIC-RFC9001-S6P6-0006.cs
- benchmarks/QuicStatelessResetBenchmarks.cs

## Data and State Considerations

The retained-route matrix depends on the endpoint's route binding tables, retained stateless-reset bindings by route length, and per-remote-address emission counters. Path identity stores both remote address and remote port, so the response floor stays tied to the same endpoint tuple rather than a token alone. The runtime continues to own the token snapshot, retained route, and loop/rate gates after AEAD-limit terminal discard; the host shells should not reconstruct a separate eligibility model.

## Edge Cases and Constraints

- Packets that do not resolve to a retained route remain suppressed.
- A retained route must match the same remote address and remote port as the retained binding.
- Loop-prevention still suppresses responses when the helper cannot keep the reset shorter than the triggering packet.
- Rate-budget exhaustion suppresses subsequent resets for the same remote address.
- Short-header and long-header packets both use the same retained-route lookup boundary.
- Direct endpoint emission and host-shell forwarding must remain consistent with the same helper-owned route/token state.

## Alternatives Considered

- Allow any later packet to reach stateless-reset emission once AEAD limits are exhausted. Rejected because it would overstate the retained-route floor and hide missing lookup state.
- Introduce a separate ambiguity-detection model for post-stop routing before the runtime expresses an ambiguity state. Rejected because the current route model is exact and the missing work is a tighter retained-route floor, not a new ambiguity engine.
- Split direct endpoint emission and host receive-loop response into unrelated policies. Rejected because the existing helper already centralizes the response gates and the host shells forward through that same helper.

## Risks

- If retained-route lookup drifts away from the same remote address and port rule, post-stop responses can be emitted for the wrong path.
- If loop-prevention and emission-rate gates are separated from retained-route lookup, the response matrix can overstate how often a reset may be sent.
- If the endpoint helper and host shells diverge on retained-route ownership, the direct emission path and receive-loop path can drift apart.

## Open Questions

- If a later change adds a distinct ambiguity state to route lookup, the gap ledger should split that case out instead of broadening the retained-route floor.
